title: 1  Introduction, Setup, Legal aspects, Coding Principles and Installation
text:
1.4 Coding principles

1.4.1 Why coding / scripts

Leave data untouched
Script is reproducible and documents what has been done (unlike GUI!)
Use command line interface (CLI)



1.4.2 Principles

DRY (don’t repeat yourself)
KISS (keep is small and simple)
YAGNI (You ain’t gonna need it)
SOLID

Single responsibility for each component of code,
Open closed (there should be no need to modify code; just add)
Liskov substitution (If program works with object, should also work with subtypes of that object: rectangle and square)

Interface (Many client-specific interfaces are better than one general)
Dependency (Implementation should be dependent on main logic, not the other way round!)
crumbs: 1  Introduction, Setup, Legal aspects, Coding Principles and Installation

title: 1  Introduction, Setup, Legal aspects, Coding Principles and Installation
text:
1.5 Installation of software

1.5.1 Open Source Software

Propriety (i.e. Philips) has increased costs and you don’t know what has been done
Use code and alter to suit particular application
Others can see what you have done, open / transparent and improves research by dialogue
use csv, or tsv; not xlsx!



1.5.2 Software Conflicts

Different versions for different tasks
Conda environments
Python environments



1.5.3 Installation
Install the software to help with the course.


1.5.4 Software used in Course

Install Miniconda and create an environment
Create a conda environment if required
Install Quarto
Install Microsoft Visual Studio
Install Python version 3.12
How to install Python packages

numpy numerical computing
scipy advanced scientific stats
pandas dataframes
scikitlearn model evaluation; confusion matrices etc
openslide reads whole pathology slide images (svs etc)
scikit-image Standard image processing
tifffile processing of large medical tiffs
imagecodecs for decoding compressed medical images
matplotlib basic plotting
seaborn statistical visualisation
plotnine grammar of graphics (ggplot2 style)
plotly interactive plots for Quarto
jupyterlab engine for notebooks / quarto
pyqt=5 for labelImg UI
lxml for labelImg XML
ultralytics for YOLO
huggingface_hub for yolo 10+c
opencv-python image processing
labelImg / https://github.com/HumanSignal/labelImg annotation software
Pillow # image processing
Python-gdcm # needed for pydicom
Pydicom # to read dicom files

Install QuPath to view digital histopathology slides
Install Zotero reference manager

title: 1  Introduction, Setup, Legal aspects, Coding Principles and Installation
text:
1.7 Excercise
The NHS number is a 10 digit number where the 10th digit is internally validated using a modulus-11 algorithm. This is done in five steps:

Each number is weighted

The first number is multiplied by ten, the second by nine, the third by eight, the fourth by seven, the fifth by six, the sixth by five, the seventh by four, the eighth by three and the ninth by two.

The results of each multiplication are added
The sum is divided by 11 and the remainder is found
The remainder is subtracted from 11 to give the check digit

If the result is 11, the check digit is 0
If the result is 10, the NHS number is invalid and not used

Check the remainder matches the check digit

For example: 987 123 4562

9x10 + 8x9 + 7x8 + 1x7 + 2x6 + 3x5 + 4x4 + 5x3 + 6x2
the sum is 295
295 : 11 = 26 remainder 9
the check digit is 11 - 9 = 2
the NHS number is valid

There are online checkers you can use
Write a Python function that checks if a NHS number if valid. The function should take a single input (argument), remove spaces or hyphens, check it is 10 digits long and output ‘valid’ or ‘invalid’ as appropriate.
crumbs: 1  Introduction, Setup, Legal aspects, Coding Principles and Installation

Directory

Much faster, but more limited life span

title: 2  Hardware, Operating Systems, Scanners, File Formats, Software
text:
2.2 Operating Systems

2.2.1 Windows

2.2.1.1 Windows Subsystem for Linux
You can run Linux on a Windows machine using Windows Subsystem for Linux, using Windows and Linux side by side. <https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install.



2.2.2 Linux
Can run on the same hardware as Windows machines, but the operating system is different and based on Unix.The Unix philosophy has small specialised programs that do one thing extremely well and can be linked together using ‘pipes’ to perform mor complex task. There is a hierarchical file system that treats devices as files, making the environment more powerful.


2.2.3 Apple (OSX)
Apple has a Unix operating system, similar to Linux.

2.2.3.1 Intel
The Intel processor (x86-64 architecture ) is used on older machines.


2.2.3.2 Apple Silicon (ARM; M processors)
More recently, Apple developed its own M series (M1 - M5) using RISC architecture focusing on efficiency, power life and high-performance per watt. The Apple Silicon chips integrate the CPU, GPU and RAM together for faster communication (whilst these are separate on Intel systems). Intel based applications can only run on a Apple processor via Rosetta 2 translation.
crumbs: 2  Hardware, Operating Systems, Scanners, File Formats, Software

title: 2  Hardware, Operating Systems, Scanners, File Formats, Software
text:
2.3 Scanners
Several scanners are available to scan histology slides. Need to name different ones. Files can be very large and this is dependent on the amount of tissue on the slide. Can easily be 3 - 4 GByte.

2.3.1 Pyramid file structure
For example the 3DHistech scanners create mrxs files. An example is included. The advantage is that not the whole slide will need to be downloaded to see a particular patch, but zoom in to what you want to see (ie. google maps). This is particularly useful for diagnostics.


2.3.2 Singe file
Like TIFF and SVS. An example is included. The whole file will need to be downloaded for viewing. The file structure is simpler and this file structure is more suited for machine learning / artificial intelligence where all pixels are required.


2.3.3 DICOM
Standard, but not universal
crumbs: 2  Hardware, Operating Systems, Scanners, File Formats, Software

title: 2  Hardware, Operating Systems, Scanners, File Formats, Software

2.4.1 Qupath
QuPath should be installed with the installer.


2.4.2 Openslide
Openslide is a Python package that can be used to import files.


2.4.3 LabelImg
LabelImg / https://github.com/HumanSignal/labelImg is software to make simple annotations

title: 2  Hardware, Operating Systems, Scanners, File Formats, Software
text:
2.5 Excercise
Open the svs file that is included in the course in Qupath. Select a region of interest (ROI) and annotate some osteoclast like giant cells. Export this region of interest as a geojson file. Import the svs file and geojson file in python and show the region of interest. Save this file as a jpg. Also, using the [create_patches.py]{https://github.com/coolpaul/msc_comp_path/tree/main/code} script you have downloaded to your computer, divide the file into patches. Bonus: stich the file patches back together again.

3.1.3 Dates
Parsing dates is often difficult due to difference in notation: 05/11/2026. In America, this is regarded as 11th May, whilst this is 5th November in UK. In similarity with numbers, it is recommended to use the ISO 8601 standard for dates. The most significant number is on the left.

title: 3  Data, Storage, Backup, Databases, Dataframes
text:
3.5 Dataframes

3.5.1 Data types

Numerical

Integer
Floating-point number
Complex number

Collection data types

Sequential collections

String (text)
List
Tuple

Unordered collection

Set
Dictionary

Boolean




3.5.2 Pandas
Pandas stores data in a dataframe. Every dataframe can have one or more variables. A variable can only contain one category (number, categorical, etc), but there can be many variables in a dataframe each of a different type.


3.5.3 Tidy data
Please refer to Hadley Wickham’s paper Wickham (2014) Tidy Data or https://r4ds.had.co.nz/tidy-data.html.


3.5.4 Grammar of graphics
If data are tidy, building plots is usually straight forward using the grammar of graphics.
Familiarise yourself with the grammar of graphics to build plots Wickham (2010). A plot is a graphical representation of the data. It requires a minimum of three elements:

a tidy dataframe
aesthetic elements
geometric element(s)

The dataframe should be in tidy format to allow ggplot (R) or plotnine (Python) to build the plot. The dataframe contains variables that could be numerical or categorical. The geometric element describes what type of plot is required (histogram, regression plot, box and whisker plot) and is related to the aesthetic element. The aesthetic element describes how the data is mapped on the plot. For example, a histogram only requires a x-axis, a regression plot an x- and y-axis, a box and whisker plot a categorical x-axis and a numerical y-axis. Additionally, variables can be allocated to colour, fill, size, transparancy etc. python
crumbs: 3  Data, Storage, Backup, Databases, Dataframes

title: 5  Objects, Labelling, GeoJSON, Annotation formats
text:
5.3 GeoJSON
File format (open-standard), based on JSON for encoding geographic data. Rules - coordinate order: [longitude, latitude] - polygon is an array of coordinates, where the first and last are identical - supported types - Point - LineString - Polygon - MultiPoint - MultiLineString - MultiPolygon - GeometryCollection
crumbs: 5  Objects, Labelling, GeoJSON, Annotation formats

5.4.1 COCO
Absolute pixel coordinates: [xmin, ymin, width, height]


5.4.2 Pascal VOC
Absolute pixel coordinates: [xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax]


5.4.3 YOLO
Normalised (0-1) coordinates: [xcenter, ycenter, width, height]

7.2 Model evaluation

7.2.1 Ground Truth
What is correct?


7.2.2 IOU

IOU (intersection over Union)
mean Average Precision (MAP)



7.2.3 Performance metrics

Accuracy
Precison (positive predictive value)
Recall (sensitivity)
no specificity in object detection!

In object detection: TP, FP and FN, but no TN! - dependent on - confidence - object overlap (50%, or more)
F1: harmonic mean precision and recall F2: more importance on recall


7.2.4 ROC curve
Explain and can be used to set the best confidence level


7.2.5 Other metrics
For numerical performance: RMSE


7.2.6 Confidence intervals
Not a probability!

title: 8  Reference Manager, Quarto, Reproducible Research
text:
8.1 Reference manager
In Zotero, create a collection to store your references for the project. Make sure the Better BibTeX plugin for Zotero is installed. Right click on the collection in Zotero and select ‘export collection’. Next, select Better BibTeX and make sure the ‘keep updated’ box is ticked and save to the project directory as references.bib. This makes sure the file remains updated when you add references to Zotero, without having to ‘re-export’ the collection.
You can cite a paper by using the Better BibTex identifier (i.e. wickhamTidyData2014) after an @ sign Wickham (2014).
crumbs: 8  Reference Manager, Quarto, Reproducible Research

title: 8  Reference Manager, Quarto, Reproducible Research
text:
8.2 Quarto project
To create Quarto project: https://quarto.org/docs/books.

-> file -> New File -> Quarto Project -> Book (or website etc..)
You can edit the _quarto.yml file as desired

To open the the project:

Open the directory in MS Visual Studio

To render the whole book:

run ‘quarto render’ in terminal

To render a single qmd file:

open the file and ‘CMD+Shift+K’
